raw_case:
Adrenal Oncocytic Pheochromocytoma: Insights From a Challenging Diagnostic Journey
Pheochromocytomas are rare adrenal medulla tumors originating from chromaffin cells, accounting for 10% of primary adrenal neoplasms. Oncocytic variants of pheochromocytomas are rare and have been reported in only 150 cases. This report describes the case of a 60-year-old female who arrived with a non-functional adrenal tumor. This case report emphasizes the importance of a comprehensive histological and immunohistochemical study for diagnosing this rare diagnostic entity and its potential diagnostic pitfalls.

Introduction

Pheochromocytomas are rare adrenal medulla tumors that arise from chromaffin cells. These tumors originate from the embryonic neural crest and approximately 10% of primary adrenal neoplasms are pheochromocytomas [1]. Specific anatomic areas with histologic and ultrastructural features characteristic of their catecholamine-secreting tumor type include big polygonal cells, thick granular eosinophilic cytoplasm, and numerous mitochondria packed firmly into the cell. Since the first instance was identified in 1997 and given the name oncocytic pheochromocytoma, there have been few occurrences of the condition recorded [2].Oncocytic tumors have been documented in several organs, including the kidney, thyroid, parathyroid, salivary glands, and pituitary [3]. Their histology demonstrates nesting, alveolar, and trabecular patterns [4]. Neuroendocrine markers such as chromogranin and synaptophysin are detected positively by immunohistochemistry [4,5].Adrenal oncocytic tumors are extremely uncommon and only approximately 150 cases have been reported [6]. Of these, six cases were oncocytic tumors of the adrenal medulla: five pheochromocytomas and one adrenal medullary oncocytoma [7]. Since oncocytic forms of adrenal pheochromocytomas are exceedingly uncommon [8], they have not been included in recent research on adrenal oncocytic neoplasms [9].

Case presentation

A 60-year-old female experienced left-side flank discomfort, weight loss, and intermittent burning during micturition for the past three months. She had a right-side nephrectomy 10 years ago for a non-functioning kidney with staghorn calculus and mild hypertension. An ultrasonographic examination revealed a cystic lesion along the medial side of the left kidney with internal echoes, raising suspicion of either a renal parapelvic cyst or an adrenal cyst (Figure 1).A CT contrast revealed a 71 x 61 x 56 mm peripherally enhanced hypoattenuating cystic left adrenal mass in the retroperitoneum near the tail of the pancreas (Figure 2). The right kidney was not visualized, which was consistent with a history of nephrectomy.Left side simple renal cortical cyst with parapelvic cyst was identified. No other masses or metastatic deposits were found (Figure 3). The patient was diagnosed with hypertension (blood pressure near 140-160/80-90 mm Hg) for four years and has been effectively managed with medication. The pre-operative biochemical evaluation showed normal adrenalin and nor-adrenalin levels in urine and plasma (Table 1). With a clinical diagnosis of a non-functional adrenal cystic lesion, surgical excision was performed.A gross examination of the received specimen revealed a well-circumscribed, cystic, solid adrenal mass measuring 75 x 60 x 30 mm and weighing 15 grams. The external surface was grayish-white and covered with yellow fibrofatty tissue (Figure 4). The cut surface was cystic with a shiny thinned-out wall; a few thickened areas in the wall of the cyst were noted, which appeared yellow; and a few hemorrhagic regions were also noted.Histopathological findings showed adrenal tissue and a cystic tumor. The wall of which comprised of nests separated by thin fibrovascular stroma (Figure 5). The cells were polygonal and showed mildly pleomorphic nuclei with small nucleoli and abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm. There was no evidence of necrosis or increase in mitotic activity (Figure 5). In this case, adrenal cortical adenoma, adrenal cortical cancer, medullary oncocytoma, adrenal cortical cyst, pheochromocytoma, and oncocytic pheochromocytoma were the differential diagnoses for adrenal oncocytic pheochromocytoma. A provisional diagnosis of pheochromocytoma/oncocytic pheochromocytoma was offered by correlating clinicopathological and immunohistochemistry findings.Immunohistochemical markers like vimentin (Figure 6), synaptophysin (Figure 7), S100 (Figure 8), and pan CK (Figure 9) were positive in sustentacular cells. These findings confirmed the diagnosis of a cystic variant of oncocytic pheochromocytoma. There was no incident throughout the recovery time. The patient has not reported any new problems throughout the last five months of routine periodic follow-up. Her abdominal discomfort has lessened, and now she has resumed her normal activities.

Discussion

Oncocytic pheochromocytoma is clinically nonfunctional and has an unclear cause. Oncocytic change is a degenerative phenomenon where mitochondria accumulate to compensate for oxidative phosphorylation uncoupling, leading to nonfunctional oncocytic tumors [10]. The literature provides six previous reports of oncocytic pheochromocytoma (Table 2) [2,4,5,10-12].In this case, the differential diagnosis among adrenal cortical cyst, pheochromocytoma, and oncocytic pheochromocytoma was crucial. Initially, a diagnosis of cortical cystic pheochromocytoma was proposed; however, immunohistochemistry findings ruled out any cortical components [5]. An immunohistochemical study indicated that this tumor was pheochromocytoma because of the neuroendocrine markers, such as vimentin, synaptophysin, S100, pan CK, and CD-56. Adrenal cortical tumors frequently test positive for SF-1 and inhibin. However, SF-1 is not widely available or used, but inhibin-negative immunohistochemistry can be observed in some adrenocortical tumors [13]. Table 2 summarizes the characteristics of oncocytic pheochromocytoma from the literature.Documented oncocytic pheochromocytoma patients do not show signs of excessive adrenaline, such as palpitations, flushing, diaphoresis, headaches, or hypertension. In three cases, hypertension was not caused by excess catecholamines. Chemical workups for pheochromocytomas showed no significant increase in catecholamines or their metabolites, indicating that oncocytic pheochromocytomas are "silent" and nonfunctional. These patients had an excellent prognosis and the tumors were clinically nonfunctional, according to the previously published research results (Table 2). Histologically, neither capsular nor lympho-vascular invasion was present, nor was there an increase in mitosis. It was difficult to identify the type of tumor - benign or malignant - until a thorough microscopic examination showed patches of hemorrhage, coagulation necrosis, and fibrotic degradation.Oncocytic tumors share cytological characteristics such as rich eosinophilic granular cytoplasm and central or eccentric nuclei. The malignancies can be distinguished using immunohistochemical investigations. For instance, pheochromocytomas are specific for chromogranin A and CD56, while an adrenocortical origin would be suggested by positive for calretinin, MART-1, and inhibin A. Synaptophysin levels can be elevated in both pheochromocytomas and adrenal cortical tumors [14].Pheochromocytoma's aggressive potential is difficult to predict. Two major scoring systems exist Thompson's PASS, based on histological criteria, and Kimura's Japanese scoring system, which includes Ki-67 immunoreactivity and catecholamine types, based on histological criteria and Ki-67 immunoreactivity [15]. PASS score includes histopathological characteristics like large nests, diffuse tumor necrosis, high cellularity, cell monotony, spindling, mitotic figures, extension into adipose tissue, vascular and capsular invasion, profound nuclear pleomorphism, and nuclear hyperchromasia for determining the aggressive nature of the lesion. A score of more than 4/20 is considered malignant [16]. The current case of pheochromocytoma is benign, showing large nests or diffuse growth exceeding 10% resulting in a PASS score of less than four.

Conclusions

Oncocytic pheochromocytomas are usually non-functional tumors with unique histological and ultrastructural features. They are often found incidentally and are differentiated from other adrenal gland tumors like adrenocortical oncocytomas and metastatic oncocytic tumors. Immunohistochemistry confirms the diagnosis by demonstrating neuroendocrine markers. This case underscores the need for a thorough diagnostic strategy for detecting and managing rare adrenal tumors, including accurate differentiation from other adrenal neoplasms and diligent follow-up.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 60-year-old female.
- Chief Complaint: Left-side flank discomfort, weight loss, and intermittent burning during micturition for the past three months.
- History of Present Illness: Long-standing mild hypertension (4 years, managed) without pheochromocytoma-specific symptoms (e.g., palpitations, flushing, headaches).
- Past Medical History: Prior right-side nephrectomy 10 years ago for a non-functioning kidney with staghorn calculus.
- Personal History: None provided.
- Family History: None provided.
- Physical Examination: None detailed in the report.
- Ancillary Tests: 
  - Ultrasonography: Left adrenal cystic lesion suspected near the left kidney.
  - CT: 71 x 61 x 56 mm peripherally enhanced hypoattenuating cystic left adrenal mass, no evidence of metastatic disease.
  - Laboratory: Normal plasma and urine adrenalin/noradrenalin levels.
  - Immunohistochemistry: Positive staining for vimentin, synaptophysin, S100, and pan CK confirmed neuroendocrine origin; inhibin-negative.
  - Histopathology: Confirmed adrenal tissue with eosinophilic granular cytoplasm, nests separated by fibrovascular stroma, and no necrosis or increased mitotic activity.
- Surgical Findings: A well-circumscribed cystic adrenal mass measuring 75 x 60 x 30 mm was excised. Gross features showed cystic areas with yellow and hemorrhagic regions.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations**:
   - Non-functional adrenal tumors: Likely based on normal biochemical tests and absence of catecholamine-related symptoms.
   - Potential diagnoses included adrenal cortical adenoma, adrenal cortical cancer, adrenal cortical cyst, pheochromocytoma, medullary oncocytoma, and oncocytic pheochromocytoma.

2. **Key Diagnostic Steps**:
   - **Histological Assessment**: Presence of eosinophilic granular cytoplasm with nests of cells supported the suspicion of pheochromocytoma or adrenal oncocytoma.
   - **Immunohistochemical Staining**:
     - Positive neuroendocrine markers (vimentin, synaptophysin, S100, pan CK) ruled in pheochromocytoma and excluded cortical tumors.
     - Absence of SF-1 and inhibin immunoreactivity excluded adrenocortical tumors.
     
3. **Excluding Other Diagnoses**:
   - Adrenal cortical tumors (e.g., adenoma/cancer): Excluded by negative inhibin/SF-1 staining and histopathological findings.
   - Medullary oncocytoma: Excluded based on confirmatory neuroendocrine marker positivity typical of pheochromocytoma.

4. **Final Conclusion**:
   - Diagnosis confirmed as an oncocytic pheochromocytoma, a rare, non-functional adrenal tumor with distinct histological and immunohistochemical findings.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Cystic oncocytic pheochromocytoma of the adrenal gland.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by going through the case step by step. The patient is a 60-year-old female with left flank discomfort, weight loss, and burning during urination for three months. She had a right nephrectomy 10 years ago for a non-functioning kidney with a staghorn calculus. Now, imaging shows a left adrenal cystic mass, and the histopathology and immunohistochemistry results are in.

First, her chief complaints. Left flank pain could be related to the adrenal mass found. Weight loss can be a red flag for malignancy, but it's non-specific. Burning on urination might suggest a UTI, but she's had this for three months intermittently, so maybe chronic or something else. However, the key here is the adrenal mass.

She has a history of hypertension managed for four years. Pheochromocytoma is a consideration here because they can present with hypertension, but her catecholamines (adrenaline/noradrenaline) are normal, which makes pheo less likely. Also, she doesn't have the classic symptoms like palpitations or headaches.

Looking at the imaging: CT shows a large left adrenal cystic mass. The size is significant (over 7 cm), and peripherally enhanced. Adrenal cysts are rare, but when they occur, they can be simple cysts or cystic neoplasms. The fact that it's a cystic lesion with peripheral enhancement might suggest a neoplasm with cystic degeneration.

Now, the histopathology and immunohistochemistry. Positive for synaptophysin and S100, which are neuroendocrine markers. Vimentin and pan CK positivity could indicate epithelial origin. Inhibin-negative might rule out adrenocortical tumors, as inhibin is often positive in adrenal cortical adenomas or carcinomas. The presence of nests separated by fibrovascular stroma and eosinophilic granular cytoplasm are features that might point towards a neuroendocrine tumor. No necrosis or increased mitotic activity suggests a low-grade tumor.

Surgical findings mention a well-circumscribed mass with cystic areas, yellow and hemorrhagic regions. Well-circumscribed is a good sign, but the size is large. The gross features could be consistent with a benign or low-grade malignant tumor.

Putting this all together: The key is the neuroendocrine origin confirmed by immunohistochemistry. The main adrenal neuroendocrine tumors include pheochromocytoma, paraganglioma, and adrenal neuroendocrine tumors (which are rare). However, pheochromocytoma was ruled out by normal catecholamines. Paragangliomas can be extra-adrenal but here the mass is adrenal. Also, the histo doesn't show the typical features of pheo (like Zellballen pattern, which isn't mentioned here). The negative inhibin points away from adrenocortical tumors. 

So, considering the positive neuroendocrine markers and the location, the most likely diagnosis is an adrenal neuroendocrine tumor. Since there's no necrosis or increased mitotic activity, it's probably a well-differentiated, low-grade tumor. The size is large (over 5 cm), which might suggest potential for malignancy, but the absence of necrosis and low mitotic count indicates a more benign course.

Alternative possibilities: Adrenal cortical carcinoma is a consideration, but inhibin is negative, which is often positive in cortical tumors. A metastatic lesion could be possible, but the CT showed no metastatic disease elsewhere, and the immunohistochemistry supports a primary neuroendocrine origin. 

So, the final diagnosis is likely a primary adrenal neuroendocrine tumor, possibly a well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor (NET) of the adrenal gland. Since pheochromocytoma is excluded by lab results and the markers point to neuroendocrine, this seems the best fit.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient's left adrenal mass is cystic, large (71–75 mm), and peripherally enhanced on imaging. Adrenal cysts are rare, and cystic neoplasms (e.g., pheochromocytoma, neuroendocrine tumors) or degenerated adrenocortical tumors must be considered. Normal catecholamine levels exclude pheochromocytoma.  
<step 2> Immunohistochemistry shows neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin, S100) and epithelial markers (pan CK), with inhibin negativity. This profile rules out adrenocortical tumors (which are typically inhibin-positive) and confirms neuroendocrine origin.  
<step 3> Histopathology reveals nests of cells with eosinophilic granular cytoplasm separated by fibrovascular stroma, characteristic of neuroendocrine differentiation. Absence of necrosis and low mitotic activity indicate a low-grade tumor.  
<step 4> The combination of neuroendocrine markers, histologic architecture, and exclusion of pheochromocytoma/adrenocortical lesions supports a primary adrenal neuroendocrine tumor. The large size and cystic changes align with reported features of such tumors.  

### Answer:
Adrenal neuroendocrine tumor (well-differentiated).

reasoning_eval.step: The patient's left adrenal mass is cystic, large (71–75 mm), and peripherally enhanced on imaging. Adrenal cysts are rare, and cystic neoplasms (e.g., pheochromocytoma, neuroendocrine tumors) or degenerated adrenocortical tumors must be considered. Normal catecholamine levels exclude pheochromocytoma.

Immunohistochemistry shows neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin, S100) and epithelial markers (pan CK), with inhibin negativity. This profile rules out adrenocortical tumors (which are typically inhibin-positive) and confirms neuroendocrine origin.

Histopathology reveals nests of cells with eosinophilic granular cytoplasm separated by fibrovascular stroma, characteristic of neuroendocrine differentiation. Absence of necrosis and low mitotic activity indicate a low-grade tumor.

The combination of neuroendocrine markers, histologic architecture, and exclusion of pheochromocytoma/adrenocortical lesions supports a primary adrenal neuroendocrine tumor. The large size and cystic changes align with reported features of such tumors.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial considerations indicate non-functional adrenal tumors based on normal biochemical tests and absence of catecholamine-related symptoms.
Step 2: Potential diagnoses include adrenal cortical adenoma, adrenal cortical cancer, adrenal cortical cyst, pheochromocytoma, medullary oncocytoma, and oncocytic pheochromocytoma.
Step 3: Histological assessment shows eosinophilic granular cytoplasm with nests of cells, supporting the suspicion of pheochromocytoma or adrenal oncocytoma.
Step 4: Immunohistochemical staining reveals positive neuroendocrine markers (vimentin, synaptophysin, S100, pan CK), ruling in pheochromocytoma and excluding cortical tumors.
Step 5: Negative SF-1 and inhibin immunoreactivity excludes adrenocortical tumors.
Step 6: Adrenal cortical tumors (adenoma/cancer) are excluded by negative inhibin/SF-1 staining and histopathological findings.
Step 7: Medullary oncocytoma is excluded based on confirmatory neuroendocrine marker positivity typical of pheochromocytoma.
Step 8: The diagnosis is confirmed as an oncocytic pheochromocytoma, a rare, non-functional adrenal tumor with distinct histological and immunohistochemical findings.
Step 9: Final diagnosis: Cystic oncocytic pheochromocytoma of the adrenal gland.
